original_abstract:
While improved activity has recently been reported for bimetallic iron–metal–nitrogen–carbon (FeMNC) catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acid medium, the underlying nature of the active sites and the interactions between the two metals remain poorly understood. In this study, bimetallic FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts were structurally and catalytically compared to their monometallic FeNC and SnNC counterparts. 

CO cryo-chemisorption revealed that the density of M-N$_x$ sites in FeSnNC and FeCoNC is approximately half that of the parent FeNC and SnNC materials. Despite this lower site density, the mass activity of both bimetallic catalysts is 50–100% higher than that of FeNC, a boost attributed to a significantly higher turnover frequency (TOF) in the bimetallic systems. Advanced characterization using electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy identified the coexistence of individual Fe-N$_x$ and Sn-N$_x$ (or Co-N$_x$) sites, but no evidence was found for the formation of binuclear Fe-M-N$_x$ sites. 

furthermore, $^{57}$Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy demonstrated that the bimetallic catalysts possess a higher ratio of D$_1$ to D$_2$ spectral signatures—representing two distinct Fe-N$_x$ coordination environments—compared to the parent FeNC. The results suggest that the addition of a secondary metal promotes the formation of D$_1$ sites, which are inherently associated with the observed increase in turnover frequency.
humanized_abstract:
Although enhanced ORR performance has recently been achieved for the bimetallic iron–metal–nitrogen–carbon (FeMNC) catalysts, the structural factors for the improved ORR performance in acid and the active sites formed in the bimetallic catalysts are not clearly realized. To further clarify the factors and sites, the FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts with distinct compositional elements were prepared and characterized and their ORR performance and stabilities were examined relative to the prepared FeNC and SnNC monometallic analogues.

CO cryo-chemisorption studies show that the M-N x site density is roughly halved in FeSnNC and FeCoNC compared to that of the parent FeNC and SnNC materials. However, mass activity of the bimetallic systems is 50–100% higher than that of FeNC due to higher turnover frequency. Electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy studies reveal that the bimetallic systems have a mixture of Fe-N x and Sn-N x (Co-N x) species without any indication of binuclear Fe-M-N x sites.

In addition, $^{57}$Fe Mössbauer spectra show that all bimetallic catalysts have a higher D$_1$/D$_2$ ratio (in relation to Fe-N$_x$ coordination sites) than their parent FeNC complex, while influencing D$_1$ site formation and hence the active catalytic species responsible for higher turnover frequencies.
